content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


July 26, 1991


Thomas Kathe
City Attorney
City of Costa Mesa
P.O. Box 1200
Costa Mesa, CA  92628-1200

RE:  Your Request For Advice
     Our File No. A-91-333

Dear Mr. Kathe:

     You have requested advice on behalf of Councilmember Sandra
Genis concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform
Act.1/

QUESTIONS

1.  When a public official discovers an error on her pre-
election statement, what procedure should be followed to correct
the error?

2.  What penalties are incurred and to whom are they paid
when an official negligently fails to disclose an uncollected loan
made from personal funds to support a city ballot measure?

3.  How should an uncollected loan made from personal funds
to support a city ballot measure be reported under the Act?

CONCLUSIONS

1.  An Amendment to Campaign Disclosure Statement (Form 405)
should be filed to correct errors or omissions on campaign
disclosure statements.  Form 405 should be filed as soon as
possible after the error or omission has been discovered.


_____________________________

1/  Government Code Section 81000-91915.  All Statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Thomas Kathe
Page 2

2. The appropriate enforcement official must determine
whether a violation of the Act has occurred and, if so, has the
discretion to seek appropriate penalties. Depending on the form
in which the enforcement action is brought, either the Commission
or a court makes the ultimate determination on the penalties
assessed. All penalties assessed are deposited into the general
fund of the jurisdiction assessing the penalty.

3. Loans that are made out of a candidate's personal funds
are disclosed on the allocation page of the campaign statement
(Form 490) and are not required to be carried over from year to
year. If the loan is forgiven, the candidate would be required to
disclose the forgiven loan again on the allocation page.

FACTS

Councilmember Sandra Genis discovered that her 1982 pre-
election campaign statement did not reflect a loan made from her
personal funds to purchase postage. The expenditure was an in-
kind loan to Mesa Action Committee in support of a local ballot
measure. Mesa Action Committee determined it could not repay the
loan and reported Councilmember Genis' expenditure as a
contribution.

ANALYSIS

1. Any report or statement may be amended by the filer at
any time. (Section 81004.5.) There are no penalties for filing
an amendment after a filing deadline.

The Amendment to Campaign Disclosure Statement (Form 405) is
used for amending campaign disclosure statements filed under
Government Code Sections 84200-84216.5. A separate form 405
should be used for each campaign statement which is being amended.

2. The enforcement official has the discretion to impose
fines for violation of the Act. For city agencies and single
county agencies, the district attorney of the county in which the
violation occurs is the enforcement authority. For charter
cities, the elected city attorney may act as the civil or criminal
prosecutor. (Sections 91001 and 91001.5.) Any penalties
collected under Section 91004 are paid to the general fund or
treasury of the jurisdiction. (Section 91009.) In addition, the
Commission has both administrative and civil enforcement authority
over any such violation.

3. Your letter indicates that Councilmember Genis had made
either a loan or an independent expenditure when she purchased
postage for use by the Mesa Action Committee. Section 82031
defines "independent expenditure" as:.

Thomas Kathe
Page 2

. . . an expenditure made by any person in connection with a
communication which expressly advocates the election or
defeat of a clearly identified candidate or the
qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly identified
measure, or taken as a whole and in context, unambiguously
urges a particular result in an election but which is not
made to or at the behest of the affected candidate or
committee.

Emphasis added.

According to the facts presented in your letter and our
telephone conversation of July 11, it appears that Councilmember
Genis made an in-kind loan and not an independent expenditure.

An in-kind loan made from an officeholder's personal funds is
reported on the allocation page, Part II, of the candidate's
campaign statement (Form 490). To amend her 1988 pre-election
campaign statement, Councilmember Genis should complete the
enclosed amendment Form 405 along with the allocation page. If
councilmember Genis decides to forgive the loan, she will need to
disclose the loan forgiveness again on her next campaign
statement.

We are forwarding a copy of this letter to our Enforcement
Division for review and appropriate disposition. You may contact
our Enforcement Division at (916) 322-6441.

If you have any additional questions regarding this advice,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

by: Linda Moureaux
Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures

The expenditure was an in-kind loan to Mesa Action Committee in support of a local ballot measure. Mesa Action Committee determined it could not repay the loan and reported Councilmember Genis' expenditure as a contribution.
sections.analysis:
1. Any report or statement may be amended by the filer at any time. (Section 81004.5.) There are no penalties for filing an amendment after a filing deadline.

The Amendment to Campaign Disclosure Statement (Form 405) is used for amending campaign disclosure statements filed under Government Code Sections 84200-84216.5. A separate Form 405 should be used for each campaign statement which is being amended.

2. The enforcement official has the discretion to impose fines for violation of the Act. For city agencies and single county agencies, the district attorney of the county in which the violation occurs is the enforcement authority. For charter cities, the elected city attorney may act as the civil or criminal prosecutor. (Sections 91001 and 91001.5.) Any penalties collected under Section 91004 are paid to the general fund or treasury of the jurisdiction. (Section 91009.) In addition, the Commission has both administrative and civil enforcement authority over any such violation.

3. Your letter indicates that Councilmember Genis had made either a loan or an independent expenditure when she purchased postage for use by the Mesa Action Committee. Section §2031 defines "independent expenditure" as:

Thomas Kathe  

... an expenditure made by any person in connection with a communication which expressly advocates the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or the qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly identified measure, or taken as a whole and in context, unambiguously urges a particular result in an election but which is not made to or at the behest of the affected candidate or committee.

Emphasis added.

According to the facts presented in your letter and our telephone conversation of July 11, it appears that Councilmember Genis made an in-kind loan and not an independent expenditure.

An in-kind loan made from an officeholder's personal funds is reported on the allocation page, Part II, of the candidate's campaign statement (Form 490). To amend her 1988 pre-election campaign statement, Councilmember Genis should complete the enclosed amendment Form 405 along with the allocation page. If councilmember Genis decides to forgive the loan, she will need to disclose the loan forgiveness again on her next campaign statement.

We are forwarding a copy of this letter to our Enforcement Division for review and appropriate disposition. You may contact our Enforcement Division at (916) 322-6441.
